title: Product Strategy: Meridian Analytics FY2026
content:
Meridian Analytics — Product Strategy FY2026

Market Position

Meridian Analytics occupies a distinctive position in the B2B analytics market. We serve mid-market and enterprise companies that have outgrown basic dashboarding tools but cannot justify the cost and complexity of full data-platform solutions like Palantir or Databricks. Our core value proposition is operational analytics that connects directly to business outcomes — not just charts, but answers.

As of Q1 2026, we have 340 paying customers, with an ARR of $14.2M. Our fastest-growing segment is companies with 200-2000 employees in financial services, logistics, and healthcare. Enterprise accounts (2000+ employees) represent 18% of customers but 47% of ARR.

Competitive Landscape

The analytics market continues to consolidate. Looker (Google) and Mode have moved upmarket, leaving a gap in the mid-market that we are well-positioned to fill. Sigma Computing and Hex remain our closest competitors in the collaborative analytics space, though neither has invested as deeply in automated insight generation.

Our primary differentiator is the Insight Engine — our proprietary anomaly detection and narrative generation layer that transforms raw data into plain-language explanations of what changed and why. No competitor offers this at our price point.

Key Bets for FY2026

1. Enterprise expansion: We are doubling down on enterprise. Our land-and-expand motion works — average enterprise account expands 2.3x in the first 18 months. We need to invest in SOC 2 Type II certification, SSO/SCIM provisioning, and dedicated customer success for accounts over $100K ARR.

2. Platform extensibility: Launch the Meridian App Marketplace by Q3, allowing third-party developers to build connectors and visualization plugins. This reduces our integration burden and creates ecosystem lock-in.

3. AI-native analytics: The Insight Engine is our moat. We will expand it from anomaly detection to predictive forecasting and automated root-cause analysis. Target: 60% of daily active users interacting with AI-generated insights by end of FY2026.

4. Self-serve onboarding: Reduce time-to-first-insight from 14 days to under 4 hours for mid-market customers. This requires a complete redesign of the onboarding flow, pre-built templates, and sample data environments.

Risks and Open Questions

The enterprise bet requires significant investment in compliance and security infrastructure. If we over-index on enterprise, we risk losing the mid-market agility that got us here. We need to maintain a dual-speed product organization — enterprise reliability with startup speed on new features.

The AI landscape is moving fast. If OpenAI or Google ships a native analytics copilot that works well enough, our Insight Engine differentiation could erode within 12 months. We must stay at least 6 months ahead on domain-specific analytics AI.

title: PRD: User Onboarding Redesign (v2)
content:
PRD: User Onboarding Redesign v2

Owner: Product Team / Emily Park
Status: In Review
Target: Q2 2026

Problem Statement

Our current onboarding flow takes an average of 14 days from signup to first meaningful insight. Customer interviews consistently cite onboarding as the #1 friction point. 38% of trial users never connect a data source, and of those who do, only 52% create their first dashboard within the trial period. We are losing winnable deals to competitors with simpler setup experiences.

The root causes are: (a) too many manual configuration steps, (b) no guidance on what to analyze first, (c) the empty-state experience provides no value until data is flowing.

Proposed Solution

Redesign the onboarding into a three-phase guided experience:

Phase 1 — Instant Value (Day 0): Provide a pre-loaded sample dataset and template dashboards so users can experience the product immediately. Include an interactive tutorial that walks through core features using the sample data. Goal: users see the Insight Engine in action within 10 minutes of signup.

Phase 2 — Connect Your Data (Day 0-1): Streamlined data source connection with one-click OAuth for major platforms (Salesforce, HubSpot, Stripe, Snowflake, BigQuery). Auto-detect schema and suggest relevant metrics. Reduce connector setup from 45 minutes to under 10 minutes.

Phase 3 — First Real Insight (Day 1-3): Once data is flowing, automatically generate three starter insights using the Insight Engine. Prompt the user to share one insight with a colleague to activate the collaboration loop.

Success Metrics

- Time to first insight: 14 days → 4 hours (sample data) / 3 days (real data)
- Trial-to-paid conversion: 12% → 18%
- Data source connection rate: 62% → 85%
- NPS at day 7: 22 → 40

Non-Goals

- This project does not address enterprise onboarding, which involves dedicated CSM handoff and custom data pipelines.
- We are not changing the pricing page or signup flow itself.

Dependencies

- Requires the sample dataset environment (Platform team, Q1 delivery)
- OAuth connectors for Snowflake and BigQuery (Integrations team, in progress)
- Insight Engine must support demo-mode with synthetic data (ML team, estimated 3 weeks)

title: Customer Interview Synthesis: Onboarding & Activation
content:
Customer Interview Synthesis — Onboarding & Activation
Research conducted: January 2026
Participants: 5 customers (2 mid-market, 3 enterprise)
Method: 45-minute semi-structured interviews

Key Themes

1. Time-to-Value Is the Biggest Barrier

All five participants cited the gap between signing up and getting useful output as their primary frustration. One mid-market participant said they almost churned during the trial because it took their team two weeks to see anything useful. Enterprise participants noted that the initial setup often requires involving their data engineering team, which adds weeks to the timeline.

2. Feature Discovery Is Accidental

Four of five participants were surprised when shown features they did not know existed. The Insight Engine, which is our primary differentiator, was unknown to two enterprise participants who had been customers for over a year. Feature discovery happens through peer recommendations or support conversations, not through the product itself.

3. Admin Burden Falls on One Person

In every account, one person (usually the initial champion) bears the full burden of configuration, user management, and troubleshooting. This creates a single point of failure — when that person leaves or changes roles, the account is at churn risk. Enterprise participants specifically asked for better role-based administration and delegation tools.

4. Collaboration Features Are Underused

Despite building substantial collaboration features (shared dashboards, comments, scheduled reports), usage data and interviews confirm these are rarely adopted. Participants cited two reasons: (a) they did not know the features existed, and (b) colleagues resist adopting yet another tool. The most successful collaboration pattern was scheduled email reports — low-tech but effective.

5. Pricing Clarity

Two mid-market participants expressed confusion about what was included in their tier versus what required an upgrade. One participant discovered they were paying for seats they did not need because user management was unclear. Enterprise participants were generally satisfied with pricing but wanted more predictable cost scaling.

Implications

These findings strongly support the onboarding redesign initiative. The feature discovery gap suggests we need better in-product education, not just improved onboarding. The admin burden pattern should inform our enterprise roadmap — specifically, role-based access and delegated administration.

title: Architecture Decision Record: Event-Driven Data Pipeline
content:
Architecture Decision Record #14: Event-Driven Data Pipeline

Date: February 2026
Deciders: Platform Team + Engineering Leadership
Status: Accepted

Context

Our current data ingestion pipeline uses REST polling to check for new data from connected sources every 15 minutes. This architecture has served us well at small scale, but is hitting limits: polling 340 customer data sources creates unnecessary API calls, introduces latency, and makes it impossible to offer real-time or near-real-time analytics.

Decision

We will migrate from REST polling to an event-driven architecture using a managed message queue (AWS SQS + EventBridge). Data sources will push change events, and our pipeline will process them as they arrive.

Tradeoffs Considered

Option A — Enhanced Polling: Increase polling frequency and add smart scheduling. Rejected because it scales linearly with customer count and does not solve the latency problem.

Option B — Webhook-First: Require all data sources to push via webhooks. Rejected because many enterprise data sources (legacy ERPs, on-prem databases) cannot initiate outbound connections.

Option C — Event-Driven Hybrid (Selected): Event-driven for sources that support it, with polling as a fallback for legacy sources. This gives us near-real-time for modern sources while maintaining compatibility.

Consequences

- Positive: Latency drops from 15 minutes to under 30 seconds for event-capable sources. Reduces unnecessary API calls by approximately 80%.
- Positive: Enables future real-time alerting and streaming analytics features.
- Negative: Increases infrastructure complexity. Team needs to learn event-driven patterns.
- Negative: Dual pipeline (events + polling fallback) means maintaining two code paths during transition, estimated 6-month overlap.

Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (Q2): Build event pipeline for top 5 connectors (Salesforce, HubSpot, Stripe, Snowflake, BigQuery). Run in shadow mode alongside existing polling.
Phase 2 (Q3): Migrate remaining connectors. Deprecate polling for event-capable sources.
Phase 3 (Q4): Retire polling infrastructure for all sources except legacy fallback.

title: Experiment Readout: Pricing Tier Restructure
content:
Experiment Readout: Pricing Tier Restructure
Run dates: January 6 - February 14, 2026
Owner: Growth Team / Marcus Chen

Hypothesis

Reducing the entry-level price from $299/month to $149/month and introducing a new Starter tier with limited features will increase trial-to-paid conversion for SMB customers without significant revenue dilution from existing mid-market accounts.

Methodology

A/B test on new signups. Control group (n=412) saw existing pricing: Pro at $299/mo, Business at $599/mo, Enterprise custom. Treatment group (n=398) saw new pricing: Starter at $149/mo, Pro at $349/mo, Business at $599/mo, Enterprise custom.

Both groups had identical product experiences. Test ran for 6 weeks to capture full trial-to-paid conversion cycles. Statistical significance threshold: p < 0.05.

Results

- Trial-to-paid conversion: Control 11.9%, Treatment 17.3% (p=0.02). Statistically significant.
- Average contract value: Control $347/mo, Treatment $271/mo (p=0.01). Significant decrease.
- Net new MRR from cohort: Control $17,100, Treatment $18,700. 9.4% improvement but not statistically significant (p=0.31).
- Starter tier adoption: 44% of Treatment conversions chose Starter, 39% chose Pro, 17% chose Business.
- Upgrade rate (Starter→Pro within 30 days): 8.2%

Key Findings

The lower entry point substantially increased conversion, particularly among companies with fewer than 50 employees. However, nearly half of converters chose the Starter tier, creating downward pressure on ACV. The 30-day upgrade rate is promising but too early to draw conclusions.

Notably, the Treatment group showed higher product engagement in the first two weeks — more dashboards created, more data sources connected, more Insight Engine queries. This suggests the lower price point attracts genuinely interested users, not just price-shoppers.

Recommendation

Proceed with the new pricing structure with one modification: gate the Insight Engine behind Pro tier to create a stronger upgrade incentive. Monitor the Starter-to-Pro upgrade funnel closely for 90 days before making the pricing permanent.

Note: This experiment focused on SMB acquisition. It does not address enterprise pricing, which remains custom and negotiated.

title: Competitive Analysis: Analytics Market Q1 2026
content:
Competitive Analysis: Analytics Market Q1 2026

This analysis covers our three closest competitors in the mid-market collaborative analytics space.

1. Sigma Computing

Positioning: Cloud-native analytics with spreadsheet-like interface. Strong appeal to business users who think in spreadsheets.
Strengths: Excellent Snowflake integration, familiar UX for non-technical users, strong enterprise security posture.
Weaknesses: Limited automated insight capabilities, no anomaly detection, visualization options are basic compared to dedicated BI tools.
Recent moves: Launched AI-assisted query building in Q4 2025. Early reviews suggest it is competent but generic — not domain-specific.

2. Hex

Positioning: Notebook-style analytics that bridges the gap between analysts and data scientists.
Strengths: Powerful for technical users, excellent collaboration on analysis notebooks, growing community and template library.
Weaknesses: Steep learning curve for business users, poor mobile experience, limited pre-built connectors. Requires SQL or Python knowledge.
Recent moves: Raised Series C at $425M valuation. Investing heavily in enterprise features and hiring a sales team for the first time.

3. Lightdash

Positioning: Open-source BI built on dbt. Appeals to data teams already using the modern data stack.
Strengths: Strong dbt integration, active open-source community, lower cost for self-hosted deployments.
Weaknesses: Requires dbt adoption (limits addressable market), visualization capabilities lag behind commercial tools, limited customer support.
Recent moves: Launched a managed cloud offering. Pricing is aggressive — free tier up to 5 users.

Meridian Differentiation

Our Insight Engine remains our strongest differentiator. None of these competitors offer automated anomaly detection with natural-language explanations at our depth. Sigma's AI query builder and Hex's notebook approach serve different needs — they help users ask questions, while our Insight Engine proactively surfaces answers.

Gaps to Address

- Sigma's spreadsheet UX is genuinely easier for business users than our dashboard builder. We should invest in a simplified exploration mode.
- Hex's community and template library creates stickiness we lack. The planned App Marketplace could address this.
- Lightdash's dbt integration is deeper than ours. We need to improve our dbt connector before losing data teams that evaluate both.

Strategic Implication

We are well-positioned in the mid-market, but the window is narrowing. Sigma is moving downmarket from enterprise, Hex is moving upmarket from technical teams, and Lightdash is growing from the open-source base. Our best defense is doubling down on the Insight Engine and ensuring onboarding is dramatically simpler than any competitor.

title: PRD: Public API Rate Limiting
content:
PRD: Public API Rate Limiting

Owner: Platform Team / Jordan Lee
Status: Approved
Target: Q2 2026

Problem Statement

Our public API currently has no rate limiting. As API usage grows (currently 12M requests/day across all customers), we face two risks: (a) a single misbehaving client can degrade performance for all customers, and (b) we have no mechanism to differentiate API access by pricing tier. Three incidents in Q4 2025 were caused by customers running unthrottled batch jobs that saturated our API servers.

Requirements

Tier-based rate limits:
- Starter: 100 requests/minute, 10K requests/day
- Pro: 500 requests/minute, 100K requests/day
- Business: 2000 requests/minute, 500K requests/day
- Enterprise: Custom, negotiated per contract

Implementation must use a sliding window algorithm (not fixed window) to prevent burst-at-boundary attacks. Rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset) must be included in every API response.

Constraints

- Must not add more than 5ms of latency to any API call
- Must work across our multi-region deployment (us-east, eu-west)
- Must be backwards compatible — existing API clients should not break
- Rate limit state must survive server restarts (Redis-backed)

Rollout Plan

1. Deploy rate limiting in monitor-only mode (log violations, do not enforce) for 2 weeks
2. Notify all API consumers via email and in-app banner 30 days before enforcement
3. Enforce Starter and Pro limits first (least disruption)
4. Enforce Business limits 2 weeks later
5. Enterprise limits coordinated individually with account managers

Monitoring

- Dashboard tracking rate limit hits by customer, endpoint, and tier
- Alert if any customer exceeds 80% of their limit consistently
- Weekly report on API usage patterns to inform future limit adjustments

title: Quarterly Retrospective: Q1 2026
content:
Quarterly Retrospective: Q1 2026
Facilitator: Sarah Kim, VP Product
Date: April 2, 2026

What We Shipped

1. Insight Engine v2.1: Improved anomaly detection accuracy by 23%. Added support for time-series forecasting (beta). On track with the AI-native analytics strategy.

2. Snowflake Direct Connect: One-click Snowflake integration, reducing setup time from 2 hours to 15 minutes. Already adopted by 34 customers.

3. SOC 2 Type II Certification: Completed after a 4-month audit. Unblocks several enterprise deals in the pipeline.

4. Mobile Dashboard Viewer: Read-only mobile app for iOS and Android. Downloads are modest (800 installs) but enterprise customers specifically requested this.

What We Deferred

1. App Marketplace: Pushed from Q1 to Q3. The team underestimated the complexity of building a secure plugin sandbox. We chose to delay rather than ship an insecure v1.

2. Role-Based Access Controls: Deprioritized in favor of SOC 2 completion. Enterprise customers are asking for this — it is now the #1 requested feature. Targeting Q2.

3. Slack Integration: Cut from the roadmap entirely for H1. Customer demand exists but is not as strong as initially estimated. Will reassess for H2 based on usage data from the collaboration features audit.

What We Learned

1. Enterprise deals require security credentials before technical evaluation begins. SOC 2 should have been prioritized 6 months earlier. Two deals worth a combined $380K were delayed by the certification timeline.

2. The pricing experiment (see experiment readout) showed strong SMB interest at lower price points. This creates a strategic tension: the strategy memo emphasizes enterprise focus, but our growth data shows SMB is a faster acquisition channel. We need to resolve this tension in Q2 planning.

3. Our onboarding NPS (22) is dragging down overall NPS (41). The onboarding redesign is not optional — it is existential for our growth trajectory.

4. Technical debt in the data pipeline is slowing feature delivery. The event-driven architecture migration is the right long-term investment, but the 6-month dual-pipeline overlap will reduce feature velocity in Q2-Q3.

Priorities for Q2 2026

1. Onboarding redesign (ship Phase 1 and Phase 2)
2. Role-based access controls
3. API rate limiting
4. Event pipeline Phase 1 (shadow mode)
5. Begin App Marketplace technical design

title: User Research: Enterprise Buyer Journey
content:
User Research: Enterprise Buyer Journey
Research conducted: February 2026
Method: Interviews with 4 enterprise buyers (VP/Director level) + analysis of 12 closed-won and 8 closed-lost enterprise deals

Buyer Personas

Primary decision maker: VP of Analytics or VP of Business Intelligence. Cares about total cost of ownership, integration with existing data stack, and vendor stability.

Technical evaluator: Senior data analyst or analytics engineer. Tests the product hands-on during POC. Cares about query performance, data modeling flexibility, and API quality.

Executive sponsor: CFO or COO who approves budget. Rarely interacts with the product directly. Cares about ROI narrative and competitive differentiation.

Purchase Timeline

Typical enterprise deal cycle: 3-6 months from first contact to signed contract.
- Month 1: Discovery and initial demo. Buyer compares 3-5 vendors.
- Month 2: Technical POC with 2-3 shortlisted vendors. Our win rate at this stage is 62%.
- Month 3-4: Security review, procurement negotiation, legal review.
- Month 5-6: Final approval and contract signing. Delays are almost always on the buyer side (budget cycles, reorgs, competing priorities).

Common Objections

1. Security posture: Prior to SOC 2 certification, this was our #1 deal blocker. With SOC 2 now complete, we expect this to diminish, though some industries (healthcare, financial services) require additional certifications (HIPAA, SOX).

2. Integration depth: Enterprise buyers want deep, bidirectional integrations — not just read access. Our current connectors are read-only, which is a gap compared to Sigma (which offers write-back to Snowflake).

3. Vendor risk: Buyers evaluate our company size, funding, and customer base. Being a smaller vendor is perceived as a risk. Our best counter is reference customers in the same industry.

4. Customization: Enterprise accounts expect white-labeling, custom SSO configurations, and dedicated infrastructure. We can deliver SSO but not white-labeling or dedicated infrastructure today.

Why We Win

In our 12 closed-won deals, the Insight Engine was cited as the deciding factor in 9 of them. The automated anomaly detection and natural-language explanations resonate strongly with VP-level buyers who want to democratize analytics across their organizations.

Why We Lose

In our 8 closed-lost deals, the top reasons were: security posture (3, pre-SOC 2), integration limitations (3), and price (2). No deal was lost on product capability alone.

title: Feature Prioritization Framework: Meridian Product Team
content:
Feature Prioritization Framework: Meridian Product Team
Last updated: March 2026

Overview

We use a modified RICE framework with adjustments for our specific context. Every feature request, enhancement, or technical investment is scored before entering the roadmap. Scores are reviewed weekly by the product leadership team.

Scoring Dimensions

1. Reach (1-10): How many customers or prospects will this impact? We weight enterprise accounts at 3x mid-market and 5x SMB, reflecting their revenue contribution.

2. Impact (1-10): How significantly does this move the needle on our core metrics (activation, retention, expansion, or NPS)? Features that improve onboarding time-to-value get a 1.5x multiplier given our current strategic priority.

3. Confidence (0.5 - 1.0): How certain are we about the reach and impact estimates? Customer-requested features with interview data get 0.9+. Internal hypotheses without validation get 0.5.

4. Effort (1-10, inverted): Engineering weeks required, including design, implementation, testing, and documentation. 1 = 10+ weeks, 10 = less than 1 week.

Final Score = (Reach x Impact x Confidence) / Effort

Fast-Track Criteria

Some items bypass the scoring process:
- Security vulnerabilities (ship immediately)
- Contractual commitments to enterprise customers (committed delivery dates)
- Regulatory requirements
- Infrastructure stability issues affecting multiple customers

Deferral Criteria

Items are deferred (not rejected) when:
- Score is below 15 (our historical threshold for roadmap inclusion)
- The feature requires a dependency that is not yet available
- The requesting customer segment does not align with current strategic focus
- Technical debt must be resolved first to build on a stable foundation

Process

New requests enter a triage queue. Product managers score them within one week. Scores above 30 are discussed at the next weekly review. Scores between 15-30 are batched for monthly review. Scores below 15 are deferred with a documented reason.

Known Limitations

This framework has a built-in bias toward enterprise features due to the 3-5x weighting. We are aware this creates tension with our SMB growth opportunity. The Growth team has proposed an alternative scoring track for SMB-specific features, which we will pilot in Q2 2026.
